Topic 2: Iran update

[196:21]And there's not too much more to say other than we're going to continue to talk about

[196:25]the course of the war and some of the operations.

[196:35]It started on Saturday.

[196:38]And so we're about to enter the second week of the war at the tail end of Friday, Friday

[196:43]at midnight, I think we'll be in week number two.

[196:47]And what we now understand about the conflict is that Trump's intention was probably that

[196:53]he wanted to go hard and go fast, hit the Iranian regime really hard and basically kill

[197:00]enough of the leadership that eventually they would surrender, destroy enough of their equipment,

[197:05]kill enough of their leadership.

[197:14]by just cutting off the head of the snake,

[197:18]cutting off the top 50 people in the regime.

[197:24]and not have to engage in this very complicated business

[197:27]of nation building and reeducation

[197:30]and political change and all those kinds of things.

[197:37]in place just with the more compliant leader.

[197:40]That seems to have been the tactic.

[197:52]middle of the fog of war.

[197:53]So things are actually very difficult to predict.

[197:56]It's actually more difficult to predict what happens tomorrow today than what is happening

[198:01]now two years ago, because the fog of war is thick and heavy and it's all around us.

[198:12]They're not asking to negotiate as far as we know.

[198:16]They're not waving the white flag.

[198:19]They are continuing and still capable of launching ballistic missiles at Persian Gulf shipping

[198:24]and at the Gulf countries.

[198:27]The Iranian council has selected a new supreme leader and they haven't named him.

[198:32]The foreign minister goes on TV and says, if the US invades us, we'll be here.

[198:38]We'll be waiting for them.

[198:42]If the United States cannot get in and get out quickly,

[198:46]if they can't do what Trump set out to,

[198:48]which is to pound Iran, achieve a quick victory,

[198:51]and then leave, if Iran is obstinate

[199:05]from the conflict, or alternatively,

[199:07]does it have to escalate?

[199:09]Well, there are a couple of variables here, a couple of really big problems if this war

[199:13]goes on for a very long period of time.

[199:15]And we've talked about one of them, which is the missile warfare.

[199:19]So Iran's primary deterrent at this point is its ballistic missile arsenal.

[199:25]And this is the biggest threat to all of Iran's neighbors that Iran has the largest and most

[199:31]diverse stockpile of ballistic missiles.

[199:34]It has a native drone factory, native ballistic missile production facilities, and they're

[199:39]all underground.

[199:40]And these missiles can just be thrown out at the golf countries, at Israel, at US bases,

[199:51]these missiles largely cannot be intercepted, at least not economically. You can shoot down

[199:57]the missiles and you can mitigate the damage. However, our ability to intercept the missiles

[200:01]is constrained by the physics of it, the technology, but also of the economics of it.

[200:09]It is a lot harder to shoot down a ballistic missile than it is to hit an airport with

[200:16]And so what does that mean? It means for every one ballistic missile that Iran launches at

[200:21]a hotel or an airport or a ship or a base, you need maybe five interceptors to go up.

[200:27]If the rate of interception is 15 to 20% because it's a very difficult business, it's much

[200:33]easier to lob a ballistic missile than to shoot one down. Then it costs five times as much.

[200:45]But then there's the, so that's a physical technological problem.

[200:48]But then you've got the economics problem, which we've discussed also.

[200:52]We don't make as many interceptors as Iran makes ballistic missiles.

[200:57]So we need five to one, but we make them at a slower rate, not a faster rate.

[201:02]And they cost much more.

[201:08]So, the rate of production is slower, the cost is higher, you need more of them for

[201:12]every ballistic missile.

[201:14]And so what this means is you can protect some sites for a short period of time, but

[201:20]you can't protect everything for a sustained period of time.

[201:24]You can protect Israel in a very narrow conflict for two weeks.

[201:30]You can protect all of Israel's population centers for two weeks, but you can't protect

[201:35]Israel, Jordan, Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Emory.

[201:39]You can't protect 10 different countries across the whole region indefinitely.

[201:45]And so what happens at that point, if you run out of interceptors is then you have to

[201:50]pick and choose.

[201:53]And this is a big problem because the Middle East is now shut down.

[201:58]There's not very many flights going into or out of the Emirates.

[202:01]And that's a big problem because you got a ton of capital markets there.

[202:05]There's a ton of very niche banking service in a country like that.

[202:09]You have a lot of ultra high net worth individuals bringing capital into a nation like the Emirates.

[202:15]And so that's very bad for business.

[202:22]to invest their money or for their capital markets.

[202:26]If African and West Asian, South Asian countries

[202:29]are doing their banking in the Emirates,

[202:31]well, that's a very big problem

[202:33]if they can't get into and out of the country

[202:35]because the airport is being bombarded with missiles.

[202:38]They don't wanna die.

[202:39]So this might be ruinous.

[202:41]And the same goes for their tourism,

[202:43]their hotels and other civilian infrastructure.

[202:48]So this cannot go on forever.

[202:51]And the issue with these ballistic missiles,

[202:53]what's more is that you can't destroy all of them.

[202:55]We don't know where all of them are.

[202:57]We can't destroy where all of them are.

[202:59]They're highly mobile.

[203:01]And what they can do is they could disassemble them,

[203:03]ship them and reassemble them somewhere else.

[203:08]And so you can launch 2000 airstrikes as we have

[203:11]against hundreds of different targets.

[203:13]And you can pound them for weeks.

[203:22]if you run out of interceptors, we don't know how many missiles they have. We don't know

[203:27]how many interceptors we have. If you run out of interceptors, then I guess it doesn't

[203:32]matter because they could just keep, even if it's a handful of them, they can lob them

[203:35]over and that's very destabilizing.

[203:42]all this stuff on land, but they're also hitting ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

[203:48]And even if they can't hit the ships, there is enough of a threat that the ships are not

[203:53]going to take the risk.

[203:59]the crude oil in the whole world goes through the Strait of Hormuz because that's where all the oil is all the oil is

[204:06]extracted from the Persian Gulf and from the

[204:09]countries that surround the Persian Gulf from Saudi Arabia Kuwait Emirates Qatar Iran

[204:15]That's where all the oil is. That's where it's refined and then that's where it's shipped out

[204:20]And there's a narrow choke point that separates the Persian golf where the oil is from the

[204:26]Indian ocean, where it goes out into the world and it goes to China or it goes elsewhere.

[204:31]That's the straight of Hormuz.

[204:32]It's a very, very narrow choke point, naval choke point.

[204:37]And so what Iran can do is they can simply threaten to attack shipping in that narrow

[204:42]sea lane.

[204:43]And even if they don't hit those ships, and even if those ships have a guarantee from

[205:05]And what has happened so far is that the volume of shipping through the straight of Hormuz

[205:10]is down about 80% and you got about 200 shipping containers that are stuck on the wrong side

[205:17]of it.

[205:17]And they're waiting for this conflict to be over.
